name: Cabo Verde
excerpt: The Portuguese discovered and colonized the uninhabited islands of Cabo Verde in the 15th century; Cabo Verde subsequently became a trading center for African slaves and later an important coaling and resupply stop for whaling and transatlantic shipping.

quick_facts.languages: Portuguese (official), Crioulo (a Portuguese-based creole language with two main dialects)
quick_facts.religions: Roman Catholic 72.5%, Protestant 4% (includes Adventist 1.9%, Nazarene 1.8%, Assembly of God 0.2%, God is Love 0.1%), Christian Rationalism 1.7%, Muslim 1.3%, Jehovah's Witness 1.2%, Church of Jesus Christ 1%, other Christian 1.3%, other 1.2%, none 15.6%, no response 0.4% (2021 est.)
quick_facts.climate: temperate; warm, dry summer; precipitation meager and erratic
quick_facts.natural_resources: salt, basalt rock, limestone, kaolin, fish, clay, gypsum
quick_facts.major_exports: fish, refined petroleum, railway cargo containers, shellfish, garments (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: strategic location 500 km from west coast of Africa near major north-south sea routes; one of four North Atlantic archipelagos that make up Macaronesia; the others are Azores (Portugal), Canary Islands (Spain), and Madeira (Portugal)
summaries.history_summary: The Portuguese discovered and colonized the uninhabited islands of Cabo Verde in the 15th century; Cabo Verde subsequently became a trading center for African slaves and later an important coaling and resupply stop for whaling and transatlantic shipping. The fusing of European and various African cultural traditions is reflected in Cabo Verde’s Crioulo language, music, and pano textiles. After gaining independence in 1975, a one-party system was established and maintained until multi-party elections were held in 1990. Cabo Verde continues to sustain one of Africa's most stable democratic governments and relatively stable economies, maintaining a currency pegged first to the Portuguese escudo and then to the euro since 1998. Repeated droughts during the second half of the 20th century caused significant hardship and prompted heavy emigration. As a result, Cabo Verde's expatriate population -- concentrated in Boston, Massachusetts and Western Europe -- is greater than its domestic one. Most Cabo Verdeans have both African and Portuguese antecedents. Cabo Verde’s population descends from its first permanent inhabitants in the late 15th-century -- a preponderance of West African slaves, a small share of Portuguese colonists, and even fewer Italians and Spaniards. Among the nine inhabited islands, population distribution is varied. The islands in the east are very dry and are home to the country's growing tourism industry. The more western islands receive more precipitation and support larger populations, but agriculture and livestock grazing have damaged their soil fertility and vegetation. For centuries, the country’s overall population size has fluctuated significantly, as recurring periods of famine and epidemics have caused high death tolls and emigration.
summaries.economy_summary: stable, middle-income, developing island economy; strong GDP growth led by tourism sector recovery; sustained poverty reduction through PEDS II development plan; high reliance on foreign remittances and aid to finance external debt

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Cabo Verde's Catholic majority leaves very little room historically for evangelical witness on these Atlantic islands. Pray for the clear gospel of salvation by grace through faith alone to reach a nation where Catholic tradition runs deep.
prayer_points: Pray for laborers to plant independent, Bible-believing local churches across a nation with very little evangelical presence.
Pray for Cabo Verdeans practicing inherited Catholic tradition to encounter genuine, personal faith in Christ.
Pray for Cabo Verdean pastors to be trained in sound, Scripture-centered teaching.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: With nearly three-quarters of the population Roman Catholic and evangelical presence very small, Cabo Verde represents a significant gospel need. Ongoing needs include training national pastors in sound biblical doctrine and planting autonomous local churches practicing believer's baptism by immersion.
